stem:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

- The axolotl is a salamander native to lakes in Mexico.
- Unlike most amphibians, axolotls keep their larval features as adults.
- They retain feathery external gills throughout their lives.
- They can regrow lost limbs, tails, and even parts of their hearts.

The student wants to emphasize a surprising biological trait of the axolotl. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: The axolotl lives in lakes in Mexico.
The axolotl is a type of salamander.
The axolotl can regrow lost limbs, tails, and even parts of its heart.
Axolotls have feathery gills.

explanation: Choice C highlights the axolotl's ability to regrow limbs, tails, and heart tissue — the most striking and surprising biological trait in the notes. The other choices state habitat, classification, or gill anatomy, which are descriptive but not especially surprising.

stem:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

- The Great Wall of China was built over many centuries.
- Construction began as early as the 7th century BCE.
- Most of the existing wall was built during the Ming Dynasty (1368-1644).
- It stretches more than 13,000 miles in total length.

The student wants to introduce the Great Wall to an audience unfamiliar with it. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: The Great Wall of China, built over many centuries, stretches more than 13,000 miles in total length.
The Ming Dynasty lasted from 1368 to 1644.
Construction began in the 7th century BCE.
Most of the wall was built during the Ming Dynasty.

explanation: Choice A introduces the subject by name and combines its long history with its massive scale — exactly what an unfamiliar audience needs. The other choices give isolated dates or facts that assume the audience already knows what the Great Wall is.

stem:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

- Mary Shelley wrote the novel Frankenstein.
- She began writing it in 1816, at age 18.
- The novel was published in 1818.
- It is often considered one of the earliest works of science fiction.

The student wants to emphasize the youth of the author at the time of writing. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: Mary Shelley began writing Frankenstein in 1816, when she was only 18 years old.
Mary Shelley's Frankenstein is often considered one of the earliest works of science fiction.
Frankenstein was published in 1818.
Mary Shelley wrote a novel called Frankenstein.

explanation: Choice A directly states Shelley's age (18) at the time she began writing, matching the goal of emphasizing her youth. The other choices focus on genre, publication year, or authorship without mentioning her age.

stem:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

- The giant sequoia is one of the largest tree species on Earth.
- Sequoias grow mainly on the western slopes of the Sierra Nevada.
- Some individuals are more than 2,500 years old.
- The largest, General Sherman, weighs an estimated 2.7 million pounds.

The student wants to emphasize the size of a single giant sequoia. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: Giant sequoias grow on the western slopes of the Sierra Nevada.
The giant sequoia is one of the largest tree species on Earth.
Some giant sequoias are more than 2,500 years old.
The largest giant sequoia, General Sherman, weighs an estimated 2.7 million pounds.

explanation: Choice D focuses on a single tree (General Sherman) and gives a concrete weight (2.7 million pounds), directly illustrating its enormous size. The other choices describe habitat, species-level generalizations, or age rather than the size of one tree.

stem:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

- Jane Goodall is a British primatologist.
- She began studying wild chimpanzees in Tanzania in 1960.
- She observed chimpanzees making and using tools.
- Before her work, scientists believed only humans made tools.

The student wants to emphasize the significance of Goodall's tool-use observation. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: Jane Goodall is a British primatologist who studies chimpanzees.
Jane Goodall began her research in 1960 in Tanzania.
Jane Goodall's observation that wild chimpanzees made and used tools overturned the scientific belief that only humans made tools.
Jane Goodall worked with wild chimpanzees.

explanation: Choice C links Goodall's specific observation to the prior scientific belief it overturned, capturing why the finding was significant. The other choices give background biography without conveying the impact of her discovery.

stem:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

- Honeybees communicate by dancing.
- A 'waggle dance' shows other bees the direction of a food source.
- The length of the dance indicates how far away the food is.
- The dance was first decoded by Austrian scientist Karl von Frisch.

The student wants to explain what information the waggle dance conveys. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: Karl von Frisch was an Austrian scientist who studied bees.
Honeybees communicate by dancing.
A honeybee's waggle dance tells other bees both the direction of a food source and how far away it is.
The waggle dance was first decoded by Karl von Frisch.

explanation: Choice C names both pieces of information the dance conveys — direction and distance — directly answering what the waggle dance communicates. The other choices discuss the scientist who decoded the dance or state only that bees dance.

stem:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

- The Wright brothers, Orville and Wilbur, built early airplanes.
- They ran a bicycle shop in Dayton, Ohio.
- Their first powered flight took place on December 17, 1903.
- The first flight lasted 12 seconds and covered 120 feet.

The student wants to describe the specific details of the Wright brothers' first powered flight. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: On December 17, 1903, the Wright brothers' first powered flight lasted 12 seconds and covered 120 feet.
The Wright brothers built early airplanes.
The Wright brothers ran a bicycle shop in Dayton, Ohio.
Orville and Wilbur Wright were brothers.

explanation: Choice A delivers the date, duration, and distance — the specific details of the first powered flight. The other choices describe background context but none of the flight's specifics.

stem:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

- Maya Lin designed the Vietnam Veterans Memorial in Washington, D.C.
- She was a 21-year-old Yale undergraduate when her design was selected.
- Her design was chosen from more than 1,400 entries in a public competition.
- The memorial was dedicated in 1982.

The student wants to emphasize how remarkable it was that Lin's design was selected. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: Maya Lin designed the Vietnam Veterans Memorial.
Maya Lin's design, chosen from more than 1,400 entries, was selected while she was a 21-year-old Yale undergraduate.
The memorial was dedicated in 1982.
Maya Lin attended Yale University.

explanation: Choice B pairs the scale of the competition (1,400+ entries) with Lin's age and student status, making her selection sound genuinely remarkable. The other choices state isolated facts that don't convey how striking the outcome was.

stem:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

- The Iberian lynx is a wildcat native to Spain and Portugal.
- In 2002, only about 94 individuals remained in the wild.
- A captive breeding and rewilding program began in the early 2000s.
- By 2023, the wild population had rebounded to more than 2,000 individuals.

The student wants to emphasize the success of the recovery program. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: Thanks to a captive breeding and rewilding program, the wild Iberian lynx population grew from about 94 individuals in 2002 to more than 2,000 by 2023.
The Iberian lynx is native to Spain and Portugal.
A captive breeding program for the Iberian lynx began in the early 2000s.
The Iberian lynx is a wildcat that lives in forests and scrublands.

explanation: Choice A contrasts the 2002 population (about 94) with the 2023 population (more than 2,000) and attributes the change to the recovery program, quantifying its success. The other choices describe habitat or only mention that a program existed.

stem:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

- Hedy Lamarr was a Hollywood actress in the 1930s and 1940s.
- She was also an inventor.
- In 1942, she co-patented a 'frequency hopping' system meant to prevent jamming of torpedo guidance signals.
- The concept later became foundational to Wi-Fi and Bluetooth technology.

The student wants to highlight the lasting technological impact of Lamarr's invention. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: Hedy Lamarr was an actress who also worked as an inventor.
In 1942, Hedy Lamarr co-patented a 'frequency hopping' system that later became foundational to modern Wi-Fi and Bluetooth technology.
Lamarr's 1942 frequency-hopping patent was originally intended for torpedo guidance.
Hedy Lamarr worked in Hollywood in the 1930s and 1940s.

explanation: Choice B names the specific invention (frequency hopping) and links it to Wi-Fi and Bluetooth, showing its lasting technological impact. The other choices describe her career or the patent's original torpedo application without making the modern-tech connection.

stem:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

- Mangroves are trees that grow in coastal saltwater areas.
- They have specialized roots that filter out salt.
- Mangrove forests store up to four times more carbon per acre than typical rainforests.
- They also buffer coastlines from storm surges and flooding.

The student wants to compare the carbon storage capacity of mangroves with that of other forests. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: Mangroves grow in coastal saltwater areas and have roots that filter out salt.
Mangrove forests store up to four times more carbon per acre than typical rainforests.
Mangroves protect coastlines from storm surges and flooding.
Mangroves are important coastal ecosystems.

explanation: Choice B directly compares mangrove carbon storage to rainforests with a specific ratio (up to four times more per acre), exactly matching the goal. The other choices describe habitat, protective function, or give a vague generalization.

stem:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

- Eadweard Muybridge was a British-American photographer active in the 1870s and 1880s.
- In 1878, he used multiple cameras to photograph a galloping horse in sequence.
- His photos settled a long-standing debate by proving all four of a horse's hooves leave the ground at once.
- His sequential images are considered forerunners of motion pictures.

The student wants to explain the scientific significance of Muybridge's 1878 experiment. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: Eadweard Muybridge was a British-American photographer active in the 1870s.
Muybridge's images are considered forerunners of motion pictures.
Muybridge's 1878 sequential photographs of a galloping horse settled a long-standing debate by proving that all four hooves leave the ground at once.
Muybridge used multiple cameras to capture a horse in motion.

explanation: Choice C names the specific scientific question Muybridge settled — whether a horse's hooves all leave the ground at once — explaining the experiment's scientific significance. The other choices describe his biography, method, or cultural legacy instead.

stem:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

- The tuatara is a reptile found only in New Zealand.
- Tuataras look similar to lizards but belong to an entirely separate order, Rhynchocephalia.
- Their closest relatives went extinct about 60 million years ago.
- Tuataras are sometimes called 'living fossils.'

The student wants to explain to an audience why tuataras are called 'living fossils.' Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: Although tuataras look like lizards, they belong to an entirely separate order whose other members went extinct about 60 million years ago, which is why they are often called 'living fossils.'
Tuataras are reptiles that live only in New Zealand.
The tuatara belongs to the order Rhynchocephalia.
Tuataras are sometimes called 'living fossils.'

explanation: Choice A explains the 'living fossils' label by tying it to the extinction of the tuatara's relatives 60 million years ago, giving the audience a reason for the name. The other choices either state the label without explaining it or give unrelated facts about habitat and classification.

stem:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

- Zora Neale Hurston was an American writer and anthropologist.
- Her 1937 novel Their Eyes Were Watching God received mixed reviews at the time.
- Hurston died in relative obscurity in 1960.
- Author Alice Walker published an essay in 1975 that helped revive interest in Hurston's work.

The student wants to emphasize the shift in how Hurston's work has been received over time. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: Zora Neale Hurston was an American writer and anthropologist who died in 1960.
Their Eyes Were Watching God was published in 1937.
Alice Walker wrote an essay about Zora Neale Hurston in 1975.

Zora Neale Hurston's 1937 novel Their Eyes Were Watching God received mixed reviews during her lifetime, and Hurston died in obscurity in 1960; however, an essay by Alice Walker in 1975 helped revive interest in her work.

explanation: Choice D contrasts the mixed early reception and Hurston's obscurity at her death with the later revival sparked by Walker's 1975 essay, making the shift in reception explicit. The other choices state isolated facts without showing any change over time.

stem:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

- The emperor penguin is the largest penguin species.
- Emperor penguins breed during the Antarctic winter.
- Males incubate a single egg on their feet for about 65 days in temperatures as low as -40°C.
- During this period, males eat nothing and can lose up to 45% of their body weight.

The student wants to illustrate the extreme physical demands of the incubation period for male emperor penguins. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: During the 65-day incubation period, male emperor penguins endure temperatures as low as -40°C, eat nothing, and can lose up to 45% of their body weight.
Male emperor penguins incubate a single egg on their feet.
Emperor penguins are the largest penguin species and breed in Antarctica.
Emperor penguins breed during the Antarctic winter.

explanation: Choice A combines duration (65 days), temperature (-40°C), fasting, and weight loss (up to 45%) — the concrete details that show how physically demanding the incubation period is. The other choices describe the behavior or species in general terms without conveying the severity.

stem:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

- Octopuses are cephalopods known for problem-solving behavior.
- A 2009 study in Indonesia recorded veined octopuses carrying coconut shell halves across the seafloor.
- They reassembled the halves into portable shelters when threatened.
- This was one of the first documented examples of tool use in invertebrates.

The student wants to explain why the veined octopus behavior was scientifically significant. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: Octopuses are cephalopods that can solve problems.
The 2009 observation of veined octopuses reassembling coconut shell halves into portable shelters was one of the first documented examples of tool use in invertebrates.
In 2009, scientists observed veined octopuses carrying coconut shells.
The coconut shells served as portable shelters.

explanation: Choice B names the specific behavior and frames it as one of the first documented cases of invertebrate tool use, explaining why the observation mattered scientifically. The other choices describe the behavior without identifying its significance.

stem:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

- Architect Lina Bo Bardi was born in Italy in 1914 and emigrated to Brazil in 1946.
- Her São Paulo Museum of Art (MASP), completed in 1968, features a main gallery suspended beneath a 74-meter concrete span with no interior supports.
- Bo Bardi designed glass easels to display paintings in open space rather than on walls.
- Critics initially dismissed the easel layout; today it is widely studied as an innovation in museum design.

The student wants to emphasize how Bo Bardi's design choices at MASP changed over time in the view of critics. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: Bo Bardi designed glass easels so paintings could be displayed in open space at São Paulo's MASP, completed in 1968.
Critics initially dismissed Bo Bardi's glass-easel layout at MASP, but today the same design is widely studied as an innovation in museum design.
Lina Bo Bardi was an Italian-born architect who emigrated to Brazil in 1946 and completed MASP in 1968.
MASP's main gallery is suspended beneath a 74-meter concrete span with no interior supports.

explanation: Choice B captures both the initial dismissal and the later reassessment of the glass-easel layout, making the shift in critical reception explicit. The other choices describe the design, biography, or structural engineering without mentioning how critics' views changed.

stem:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

- Mathematician Katherine Johnson worked at NASA's Langley Research Center starting in 1953.
- Her trajectory calculations were used for the 1961 flight of Alan Shepard, the first American in space.
- For John Glenn's 1962 orbital mission, NASA had begun using electronic computers, but the computers were considered unreliable.
- Before launch, Glenn personally asked Johnson to re-verify the computer's calculations by hand.

The student wants to emphasize the level of trust astronauts placed in Johnson's work during an early shift toward electronic computing at NASA. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: Katherine Johnson calculated trajectories for the 1961 Alan Shepard flight, the first American space mission.
Katherine Johnson began working at NASA's Langley Research Center in 1953.
By 1962, NASA was using electronic computers, though early machines were considered unreliable.
Even after NASA began relying on electronic computers, John Glenn refused to launch on his 1962 orbital mission until Katherine Johnson re-verified the computer's calculations by hand.

explanation: Choice D places Glenn's personal request against the backdrop of NASA's new electronic computers, showing that an astronaut trusted Johnson's hand calculations more than the new machines. The other choices mention her career or the computers in isolation, without connecting them to astronaut trust.

stem:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

- The Svalbard Global Seed Vault, built into a mountain on a Norwegian Arctic island, opened in 2008.
- It was designed to store backup samples of the world's crop seeds in case of regional or global catastrophe.
- In 2015, researchers withdrew seeds for the first time to restore a gene bank in war-torn Syria.
- By 2017, restored seed samples from that withdrawal had been re-deposited into the vault.

The student wants to illustrate how the vault fulfilled its intended function under real-world conditions. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: The Svalbard Global Seed Vault opened in 2008 in a mountain on a Norwegian Arctic island.
The vault stores backup samples of the world's crop seeds.
The 2015 withdrawal was the first time seeds were taken out of the vault.
When a gene bank in war-torn Syria needed to be restored in 2015, researchers withdrew seeds from the Svalbard vault — the first such withdrawal — and by 2017 had re-deposited restored samples.

explanation: Choice D shows the vault's purpose in action: a real-world withdrawal to restore a Syrian gene bank, followed by re-deposit of restored samples — the full loop of its intended function. The other choices describe the vault's opening, purpose, or the withdrawal in isolation without completing the story.

stem:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

- Composer Florence Price was the first Black woman to have a symphony performed by a major American orchestra.
- Her Symphony No. 1 was premiered by the Chicago Symphony Orchestra in 1933.
- After her death in 1953, most of her manuscripts were thought to be lost.
- In 2009, a large collection of her scores, including two violin concertos, was discovered in an abandoned Illinois house she had once owned.

The student wants to emphasize how the 2009 discovery reshaped the known scope of Price's surviving work. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: Although most of Florence Price's manuscripts were thought to be lost after her death in 1953, a 2009 discovery in an abandoned Illinois house she had once owned uncovered a large collection of her scores, including two violin concertos.
Florence Price's Symphony No. 1 was premiered by the Chicago Symphony Orchestra in 1933, the first symphony by a Black woman performed by a major American orchestra.
Florence Price was a composer who died in 1953.
In 2009, a cache of Florence Price's music was found in Illinois.

explanation: Choice A contrasts the presumed loss of Price's manuscripts with the 2009 discovery and specifies what was found, directly showing how the scope of her known surviving work changed. The other choices either describe an unrelated milestone or mention the discovery too vaguely to show its impact.
